project_summary: GRIP-TK is a master thesis toolkit for creating and evaluating XR interaction techniques through a node-graph workflow. It brings interaction logic, runtime behavior, logging, and evaluation setup into one system, reducing the amount of repeated work normally needed when building XR experiments.
description:
This master thesis presents GRIP-TK, a node-graph toolkit for immersive authoring and evaluation of XR interaction techniques. The main idea was to support the repeated setup work that often appears across XR experiments, where developers need to rebuild similar interaction structures, logging systems, and evaluation pipelines from study to study.

The toolkit was implemented as a framework for composing interaction techniques together with experiment logic, visualization flow, and data collection support. It was not only meant to help create techniques more systematically, but also to make them easier to inspect, extend, and connect to concrete evaluation designs.

To demonstrate that workflow, the thesis walks through how interaction techniques can be constructed inside the system and later extended, including a parallax-mitigated node variant. GRIP-TK was then used in a controlled Fitts' Law study with five XR interaction techniques, showing that the toolkit could support both the runtime side of the experiment and the broader analysis pipeline needed to reach empirical conclusions.

A central outcome of the work is that the toolkit supports more than authoring alone. It helps connect technique construction, experiment execution, logging, and evaluation into a single workflow, while also leaving room for future usability and productivity evaluation of the toolkit itself.
project_context: Master thesis in Computer Science, Aarhus University
project_goal: Support the full XR experiment workflow by providing a node-graph toolkit for constructing interaction techniques, connecting them to experiment runtime logic, and linking them to logging and evaluation pipelines
project_technologies:
Unity
Meta XR SDK
Node-graph architecture
XR interaction techniques
Experiment runtime logic
Logging infrastructure
Visualization flow
Fitts' Law evaluation
Qualitative analysis
Extensibility design
project_takeaways:
Toolkit architecture for reusable XR experiments
Interaction technique definition through modular node structures
Experiment-to-analysis workflow integration
Extensibility thinking across node, structure, and environment levels

project_summary: BlobbyKelp is a bachelor thesis prototype for organic data visualization, representing grouped data points on maps and 2D graphs through soft, blobby shapes. The project focuses on transforming rigid geometric groupings into more readable and visually natural shapes, making clusters easier to interpret.
description:
This bachelor thesis explores how grouped data points can be visualized more organically while still staying readable and structurally meaningful.

The prototype, called BlobbyKelp, was built to move beyond rigid geometric clusters and create a softer visual language for maps and 2D graphs. It draws inspiration from GapMinder, Kelp Diagrams, BubbleSets, and KelpFusion, but aims for a more continuous and organic result.

To achieve that, the project combines graph routing, local grouping, hull generation, and curve smoothing. Related points are connected efficiently, grouped regions are shaped through convex and non-convex hull logic, and spline-based smoothing helps transform sharp boundaries into blobby forms that feel more natural to read.

A large part of the work was about balancing visual clarity with technical control. The final prototype supports animation and adjustable thresholds, making it easier to inspect how grouped data evolves and how different clustering choices affect the output.
project_context: Bachelor thesis in Computer Science, Aarhus University (15 ECTS)
project_goal: Transform plain geometric groupings into organic, readable shapes for maps and 2D graph visualizations
project_technologies:
JavaScript
SVG
Kruskal's Algorithm
Graham Scan
Non-convex hull generation
Bowyer-Watson triangulation
B-splines
Bezier curves
Catmull-Rom splines
Animation controls
Distance-threshold interaction
project_takeaways:
Computational geometry for visual grouping
Routing-based structure for readability
Smoother visual rendering through spline techniques

project_name: 3D UI Project : Multiple Frame Manipulation in VR
project_summary: Multiple Frame Manipulation in VR is a VR interaction prototype that lets users manipulate distant or occluded 3D objects through user-created frames and a tablet-based 2D interface.
description:
This project explores how frame-based viewpoints can reduce the need for physical movement in VR. Instead of walking, reaching, or constantly changing position, the user creates portable frames of different scene areas and later uses them as interaction spaces on a tablet interface.

The prototype supports viewpoint creation, frame switching, single-object manipulation, frame-to-frame object movement, and lasso-based multi-object selection. Technically, the system maps 2D tablet input back into the 3D scene using projection-based control, allowing objects to be moved from a mostly stationary position.

The project was also evaluated through user tasks comparing frame-based manipulation with standard direct manipulation. The results showed that the approach helped with distant objects, occluded objects, and multi-object movement, but also introduced extra mental demand, sensitivity to small hand movements, and challenges with precise depth control.
project_goal: Develop and evaluate a frame-based VR manipulation technique that enables users to select and move distant or occluded 3D objects without relying on physical walking, reaching, or direct line-of-sight alignment.
project_technologies:
Unity
Meta Quest 2
Meta Interaction SDK
Hand tracking
Hand pose detection
Frame-based interaction
Tablet-based 2D interface
2D-to-3D projection
Ray-plane intersection
RenderTexture
Viewpoint capture
Frame switching
Single-object manipulation
Frame-to-frame manipulation
Lasso-based multi-object selection
Unity UI Extensions
NASA-TLX
SUS-based usability evaluation
project_takeaways:
Designing VR interaction techniques for distant object manipulation
Mapping 2D tablet input into 3D object transformations
Using user-created viewpoints as reusable interaction frames
Implementing hand-tracked gesture interaction in Unity
Supporting both single-object and multi-object manipulation workflows
Evaluating trade-offs between reduced physical effort and increased cognitive load

project_name: Building the Internet of Things with P2P and Cloud Computing : AirBrush Project
project_summary: Airbrush is a multiplayer browser drawing game where players use phone motion sensors to draw in the air, while others guess the drawing on a shared canvas. The project explores WebRTC-based peer-to-peer communication and IoT-style motion interaction.
description:
Airbrush explores how a smartphone can be used as a motion-based drawing controller in a browser game. One player acts as the drawer, using accelerometer and gyroscope input from their phone to create brushstrokes on a shared canvas, while the other players guess the word being drawn.

The main technical focus was building a real-time multiplayer system without relying on a traditional central game server for all communication. The project uses WebRTC data channels for peer-to-peer synchronization, while a signaling server is only used to help peers discover each other and establish connections. This made the project relevant for understanding decentralized communication, NAT traversal, synchronization, and real-time state sharing between browsers.

The prototype also includes game-state management such as player roles, chat, scoring, round progression, word hints, and canvas synchronization. A key challenge was balancing responsiveness with noisy motion-sensor data, since phone movement had to be translated into smooth and understandable drawing input. Overall, the project combines IoT sensor interaction, browser-based game development, and peer-to-peer networking into one experimental drawing game.
project_context: Building the Internet of Things with P2P and Cloud Computing course project
project_goal: Build a multiplayer browser drawing game where a smartphone acts as a motion-based drawing controller. Explore how phone sensor data, real-time peer-to-peer communication, and shared browser canvases can support interactive drawing and guessing gameplay.
project_technologies:
WebRTC
Peer-to-peer communication
Signaling server
Socket.IO
Node.js / Express
Three.js
Accelerometer / gyroscope input
Motion-sensor controls
Shared canvas synchronization
Single-page application
Cloud hosting
Nginx reverse proxy
STUN / NAT traversal
project_takeaways:
Building real-time peer-to-peer browser communication with WebRTC
Using a signaling server for peer discovery and connection setup
Translating phone motion-sensor data into drawing input
Synchronizing canvas, chat, timer, score, and game state across peers
Handling sensor noise, latency, NAT traversal, and multiplayer state consistency

project_name: Advanced Augmented Reality Project : AR Kingdoms
project_summary: AR Kingdoms is an AR/VR tabletop strategy prototype for Meta Quest 2, where the player rules a virtual kingdom placed on a real table using hand tracking, gaze/pinch selection, direct touch interaction, and card-based building placement.
description:
This project explored how a physical table and surrounding room can be used as part of a mixed-reality strategy interface. The prototype used spatial anchors, hand tracking, gaze/pinch selection, direct touch interaction, and card-based placement to connect virtual game objects with real-world surfaces.

The design process moved through several stages: early ideation from cultural themes, sketching and concept selection, mockups, think-aloud playtesting, and a final EXPO evaluation. Each phase helped refine the interaction flow, especially how users selected buildings, interacted with citizens, and understood the system state.

A central focus was environmental feedback. Instead of showing everything through standard menus, the prototype used candles, paintings, walls, and data panels to communicate progress, happiness, resources, and political state. The EXPO feedback showed that the atmosphere and tabletop concept were effective, while also highlighting areas for improvement such as tutorial clarity, interaction mapping, and clearer communication of the project’s underlying themes.
project_goal: Explore how a physical table and surrounding room can become part of a mixed-reality strategy interface. Build an AR/VR tabletop prototype where virtual game objects are spatially anchored to real-world surfaces and controlled through hand-based interaction.
project_technologies:
Unity
Meta Quest 2
Mixed Reality Utility Kit
Spatial Anchors
Hand tracking
Gaze/pinch selection
Direct touch interaction
Card-based placement
Environmental feedback
AR/VR tabletop interaction
project_takeaways:
Designing spatial interaction for mixed-reality tabletop systems
Using real-world surfaces as part of an AR/VR interface
Combining hand tracking, gaze/pinch input, and direct manipulation
Communicating system state through environmental feedback
Iterating interaction design through mockups, playtesting, and EXPO evaluation

project_name: Deep Learning for Visual Recognition: YOLO-Style Object Detector From Scratch
project_summary: A lightweight YOLO-style model trained from scratch to recognise football players and the ball, with a focus on improving detection of the small and underrepresented football class.
description:
This project studies a simplified YOLO-inspired grid classifier for football image analysis. The model uses a compact convolutional backbone and a classification head that predicts whether each grid cell contains a player or the ball. Rather than relying on a large pre-trained object detector, the project focuses on understanding how much performance can be gained from a small model through better training strategies.

A central challenge was the strong imbalance between the two classes. Player cells appeared far more often than ball cells, meaning that the baseline model could achieve high overall accuracy while still failing to recognise the football reliably. To investigate this, we compared several approaches, including class-weighted loss, random oversampling, hybrid sampling, cyclic learning rates, and hard negative mining.

The final pipeline showed that the most effective improvements came from combining hybrid sampling with cyclic learning and hard negative mining. This made the model focus more on difficult ball examples and improved minority-class performance without sacrificing player accuracy.
project_context: Deep Learning for Visual Recognition course project
project_goal:
Build a lightweight YOLO-inspired classifier from scratch
Distinguish football players from the football in match images
Improve recognition of the football as a small minority-class object
project_technologies:
Convolutional neural networks
YOLO-style grid classification
Class imbalance handling
Hybrid sampling
Oversampling / undersampling
Class-weighted loss
Cyclic learning rates
Learning-rate range test
Hard negative mining
Confusion matrix evaluation
project_takeaways:
Training a compact CNN-based grid classifier from scratch
Handling severe class imbalance at grid-cell level
Improving minority-class performance through sampling and learning-rate strategies
Using confusion matrices to identify class-specific failure cases
Understanding the limits of lightweight YOLO-style models for small-object classification
